GLOWr 0.1.0

  • Changelog

Calculate Optimal Weights for GLOW Tests

Source: R/optimal_weights.R
Optimal_Weights_M.Rd

Main function for computing optimal weights for variant-set tests in the GLOW framework. Computes weights under different hypotheses (H0 vs H1) and approximation methods (normal vs sparse), tailored to the specific test transformation function g.

Optimal_Weights_M(g, Bstar, PI, M, is.posi.wts = TRUE)

Arguments

g

Function, transformation applied to Z-scores (e.g., identity for Burden, GFisher transformation for Fisher-type tests)

Bstar

Numeric vector, estimated effect sizes for each variant

PI

Numeric vector, estimated variant-importance scores

M

Numeric matrix, correlation matrix of marginal Z-scores

is.posi.wts

Logical, if TRUE, negative weights are set to zero (default TRUE)

Value

A list with optimal weights. The structure depends on the transformation function:

For Burden/Liptak tests (when g is identity):

wts_BE

Weights optimized for Best Effect under H0

wts_APE

Weights optimized for Average Power under Ensemble under H1

For other tests (Fisher, SKAT, etc.):

wts_BE_N

BE weights using normal approximation

wts_APE_N

APE weights using normal approximation

wts_BE_sparse

BE weights using sparse mixture approximation

wts_APE_sparse

APE weights using sparse mixture approximation

Details

This function orchestrates the optimal weight calculation for GLOW tests. The procedure differs based on the transformation function g:

For Burden/Liptak tests (g is identity):

The weights have closed-form solutions: $$wts_{BE} = MU$$ $$wts_{APE} = (I + diag(V)M)^{-1} MU$$ where \(MU = Bstar * PI\) and \(V = Bstar^2 * PI * (1-PI)\).

For other tests (GFisher, SKAT, etc.):

Two approximation methods are used:

  1. Normal Approximation: Assumes Z-scores are approximately normal under the alternative with means MMU and variances M + MVM, where:

    • MMU = M \

    • MVM = t(M * V) \

  2. Sparse Mixture Approximation: Explicitly models the sparse signal structure Z_i = Z_0i + Bstar_i * C_i where C_i ~ Bernoulli(PI_i).

For each approximation, weights are computed under two hypotheses:

  • BE (Best under H0): Assumes no signal, optimizes for power under worst-case

  • APE (Average Power under Ensemble): Accounts for signal, optimizes for average power

Transformation Function Check:

The function checks if g is the identity by comparing function bodies: identical(body(g), body(function(x) x))

Computational Complexity

  • Burden/Liptak: O(n^3) for matrix inversion

  • Other tests: O(4 * n^2 * K * n_eval + n^3) where:

    • n = number of variants

    • K = number of Hermite polynomial orders (8)

    • n_eval = cost per numerical integration

    • Factor of 4 from normal + sparse approximations, each under H0 and H1

Assumptions

  • Bstar, PI, and M have compatible dimensions

  • M is a valid correlation matrix (positive definite, diagonal = 1)

  • 0 <= PI_i <= 1 for all i

  • Bstar values are positive (standardized effect sizes)

  • For non-Burden tests, g is a smooth function suitable for Hermite expansion

Weight Interpretation

  • Larger weights indicate variants that contribute more to the test statistic

  • All weights are normalized by mean(abs(weights)) for comparability

  • BE weights are conservative (good when signal is uncertain)

  • APE weights are adaptive (good when signal estimates are reliable)

  • Sparse weights are more accurate for rare causal variants

  • Normal weights are computationally simpler approximations

Performance Optimization

The function uses several optimizations for improved performance:

  • H0 covariance matrices are cached to speed up repeated calls

  • Fast path computation for independent variants (identity correlation)

  • Caching can be controlled: options(GLOWr.use_cache = TRUE/FALSE)

  • Cache can be cleared: clear_glow_cache()

  • Cache status: glow_cache_info()

References

Zhang, H., Liu, M., Landers, J. E., and Wu, Z. Integrated Weighted Association Test with Application to Genetic Association Studies. Annals of Applied Statistics (in revision).

Examples

if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{
# Example 1: Burden test (identity transformation)
g_identity <- function(x) x
Bstar <- c(0.5, 1.0, 0.3)
PI <- c(0.01, 0.05, 0.02)
M <- diag(3)
weights_burden <- Optimal_Weights_M(g_identity, Bstar, PI, M)
names(weights_burden)  # wts_BE, wts_APE

# Example 2: GFisher test
g_GFisher_two <- function(x, df = 2) {
  qchisq(log(2) + pnorm(abs(x), lower.tail = FALSE, log.p = TRUE),
         df = df, lower.tail = FALSE, log.p = TRUE)
}
weights_fisher <- Optimal_Weights_M(
  function(x) g_GFisher_two(x, df = 2),
  Bstar, PI, M
)
names(weights_fisher)  # wts_BE_N, wts_APE_N, wts_BE_sparse, wts_APE_sparse
} # }

Contents

Developed by Hong Zhang, Ming Liu, Zheyang Wu.
